solution:

∵ System of equation has non trivial solution. It is possible only when ∆=0.
∴∆ =1-c-cc-1ccc-1=0
⇒11-c2+c-c-c2-cc2+c=0
⇒2c3+3c2-1=0
⇒c=-1, 12 

Hence, the greatest value of c=12.

subject: Mathematics
q: If $\quad y(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\sin x & \cos x & \sin x+\cos x+1 \\ 27 & 28 & 27 \\ 1 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right|, x \in \mathbb{R}$, then $\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+y$ is equal to
question: If $\quad y(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\sin x & \cos x & \sin x+\cos x+1 \\ 27 & 28 & 27 \\ 1 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right|, x \in \mathbb{R}$, then $\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+y$ is equal to
solution: $\begin{aligned} & C_3 \rightarrow C_3-C_1 \\ & y(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\sin x & \cos x & 1+\cos x \\ 27 & 28 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 & 0\end{array}\right| \\ & y(x)=-(1+\cos x) \\ & \frac{d y}{d x}=\sin x \\ & \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=\cos x \\ & \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}+y=-1\end{aligned}$

q:

x+3y-z=-18

3x-y+λ2-λz=16 has no solutions, is
question:

2x-3y+5z=9

x+3y-z=-18

solution:
For the system of linear equations

2x-3y+5z=9

x+3y-z=-18

3x-y+λ2-λz=16 to have no solutions, 

∆ should be equal to 0.

i.e. Δ=2-3513-13-1λ2-λ

=23λ2-3λ-1+3λ2-λ+3+5-1-9

=9λ2-9λ-43

=9λ2-9λ-43

Now ∆=0 for 2 values of λ as for the above quadratic the value of determinant is greater than zero.

So, 2 values of λ satisfy the system of equations to obtain no solution.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Statement 1: If the system of equations $x+k y+$ $3 z=0,3 x+k y-2 z=0,2 x+3 y-4 z=0$ has a nontrivial solution, then the value of $k$ is $\frac{31}{2}$.
Statement 2: A system of three homogeneous equations in three variables has a non trivial solution if the determinant of the coefficient matrix is zero.
question:
Statement 1: If the system of equations $x+k y+$ $3 z=0,3 x+k y-2 z=0,2 x+3 y-4 z=0$ has a nontrivial solution, then the value of $k$ is $\frac{31}{2}$.
Statement 2: A system of three homogeneous equations in three variables has a non trivial solution if the determinant of the coefficient matrix is zero.
options: Statement 1 is false, Statement 2 is true.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true, Statement 2 is a correct explanation for Statement 1.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is true,, Statement 2 is not a correct explanation for Statement 1.
Statement 1 is true, Statement 2 is false.
solution:
Given system of equations is
$$
\begin{aligned}
& x+k y+3 z=0 \\
& 3 x+k y-2 z=0 \\
& 2 x+3 y-4 z=0
\end{aligned}
$$
Since, system has non-trivial solution
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & k & 3 \\
3 & k & -2 \\
2 & 3 & -4
\end{array}\right|=0 \\
\Rightarrow & 1(-4 k+6)-k(-12+4)+3(9-2 k)=0 \\
\Rightarrow & 4 k+33-6 k=0 \Rightarrow k=\frac{33}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$
Hence, statement - 1 is false.
Statement- 2 is the property.
It is a true statement.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let the system of equations
$\begin{aligned}
& x+5 y-z=1 \\ & 4 x+3 y-3 z=7 \\ & 24 x+y+\lambda z=\mu
\end{aligned}$
$\lambda, \mu \in \mathrm{R}$, have infinitely many solutions. Then the number of the solutions of this system, If $x, y, z$ are integers and satisfy $7 \leq x+y+z \leq 77$, is
question:
Let the system of equations
$\begin{aligned}
& x+5 y-z=1 \\ & 4 x+3 y-3 z=7 \\ & 24 x+y+\lambda z=\mu
\end{aligned}$
$\lambda, \mu \in \mathrm{R}$, have infinitely many solutions. Then the number of the solutions of this system, If $x, y, z$ are integers and satisfy $7 \leq x+y+z \leq 77$, is
solution:
For infinitely many solution
$\begin{aligned}
& \Delta=0 \\ & \left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & 5 & -1 \\ 4 & 3 & -3 \\ 24 & 1 & \lambda
\end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 1(3 \lambda+3)-5(4 \lambda+72)-1(4-72)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-17 \lambda+3-4 \times 72-4=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 17 \lambda=-289
\end{aligned}$

$\begin{aligned} & \Delta 1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 5 & -1 \\ 7 & 3 & -3 \\ \mu & 1 & -17\end{array}\right|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 1(-51+3)-5(-119+3 \mu)-1(7-3 \mu)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow-48+595-15 \mu-7+3 \mu=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 12 \mu=540\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & x+5 y-z=1 \\ & 4 x+3 y-3 z=7 \\ & 24 x+y-17 z=45 \\ & \text { Let } z=1 \\ & x+5 y=1+\lambda] \times 4 \\ & 4 x+3 y=7+3 \lambda \\ & 4 x+20 y=4+4 \lambda \\ & \frac{-17 y=3-\lambda}{-17}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \begin{aligned} \mathrm{y} & =\frac{\lambda-3}{17}, \mathrm{x}=1+\lambda-\frac{5 \lambda-15}{17} \\ & =\frac{32-12 \lambda}{17} \\ 7 & \leq \frac{\lambda-3}{17}+\frac{32+12 \lambda}{17}+\lambda \leq 77 \\ 7 & \leq \frac{30 \lambda+29}{17} \leq 77 \\ 3 & \leq \lambda \leq 42 \\ \lambda & =3,20,37\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

q:
Let S be the set of all λ∈R for which the system of linear equations

2x-y+2z=2

x-2y+λz=-4

x+λy+z=4

has no solution. Then the set S
question:
Let S be the set of all λ∈R for which the system of linear equations

2x-y+2z=2

x-2y+λz=-4

x+λy+z=4

has no solution. Then the set S
solution:
For no solution Δ=0 and at least one of Δ1,Δ2,Δ3 is non-zero.
Δ=2-121-2λ1λ1=-λ-12λ+1
Δ1=2-12-4-2λ4λ1=-2λ2+6λ-4
Δ=0⇒  λ=1,-12

 And for  λ=1,-12,  ∆1≠0
Hence, S=1,-12

subject: Mathematics
q:
If a, b, c  are non - zero real numbers and if the system of equations

a-1x=y+z

b-1y=x+z

c-1z=x+y

has a non - trivial solution, then ab+bc+ca  equals :
question:
If a, b, c  are non - zero real numbers and if the system of equations

a-1x=y+z

b-1y=x+z

c-1z=x+y

has a non - trivial solution, then ab+bc+ca  equals :
solution:
Given, a-1x=y+z⇒a-1x-y-z=0

b-1y=z+x⇒-x+b-1y-z=0

c-1z=y+x=-x-y+c-1z=0

For a non-trivial solution

a-1-1-1-1b-1-1-1-1c-1=0

⇒   1-a1111-b1111-c=0

⇒   1-a1-b1-c-1-11-c-1+1-1-b=0

⇒   1-a+b+c+ab+bc+ca-abc-1+a+b+c=0

⇒ab+bc+ca=abc

subject: Mathematics
q:
If $\mathrm{f}(\theta)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & \cos \theta & 1 \\ -\sin \theta & 1 & -\cos \theta \\ -1 & \sin \theta & 1\end{array}\right|$ and
$\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ are respectively the maximum and the minimum values of $f(\theta)$, then $(A, B)$ is equal to:
question:
If $\mathrm{f}(\theta)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & \cos \theta & 1 \\ -\sin \theta & 1 & -\cos \theta \\ -1 & \sin \theta & 1\end{array}\right|$ and
$\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ are respectively the maximum and the minimum values of $f(\theta)$, then $(A, B)$ is equal to:
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text { Let } f(\theta)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & \cos \theta & 1 \\
-\sin \theta & 1 & -\cos \theta \\
-1 & \sin \theta & 1
\end{array}\right| \\
&=(1+\sin \theta \cos \theta)-\cos \theta(-\sin \theta-\cos \theta) \\
&+1\left(-\sin ^2 \theta+1\right) \\
&=1+\sin \theta \cos \theta+\sin \theta \cos \theta+ \\
&=2+2 \sin \theta \cos \theta+\cos 2 \theta \\
&=2+\sin 2 \theta+\cos 2 \theta
\end{aligned}
$$
Now, maximum value of (1)
is $2+\sqrt{1^2+1^2}=2+\sqrt{2}$ and minimum value of (1) is
$$
2-\sqrt{1^2+1^2}=2-\sqrt{2} \text {. }
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: If $a^2+b^2+c^2=-2$ and $f(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1+a^2 x & \left(1+b^2\right) x & \left(1+c^2\right) x \\ \left(1+a^2\right) x & 1+b^2 x & \left(1+c^2\right) x \\ \left(1+a^2\right) x & \left(1+b^2\right) x & 1+c^2 x\end{array}\right|$ then $f(x)$ is $a$ polynomial of degree
question: If $a^2+b^2+c^2=-2$ and $f(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1+a^2 x & \left(1+b^2\right) x & \left(1+c^2\right) x \\ \left(1+a^2\right) x & 1+b^2 x & \left(1+c^2\right) x \\ \left(1+a^2\right) x & \left(1+b^2\right) x & 1+c^2 x\end{array}\right|$ then $f(x)$ is $a$ polynomial of degree
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& f(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1+\left(a^2+b^2+c^2+2\right) x & \left(1+b^2\right) x & \left(1+c^2\right) x \\
1+\left(a^2+b^2+c^2+2\right) x & 1+b^2 x & \left(1+c^2\right) x \\
1+\left(a^2+b^2+c^2+2\right) x & \left(1+b^2\right) x & 1+c^2 x
\end{array}\right| \text {, Applying } C_1 \rightarrow C_1+C_2+C_3 \\
& =\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & \left(1+b^2\right) x & \left(1+c^2\right) x \\
1 & 1+b^2 x & \left(1+c^2\right) x \\
1 & \left(1+b^2\right) x & 1+c^2 x
\end{array}\right| \because a^2+b^2+c^2+2=0 \\
& f(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
0 & x-1 & 0 \\
0 & 1-x & x-1 \\
1 & \left(1+b^2\right) x & 1+c x
\end{array}\right| ; \text { Applying } R_1 \rightarrow R_1-R_2, R_2 \rightarrow R_2-R_3 \\
& f(x)=(x-1)^2
\end{aligned}
$$
Hence degree $=2$.

solution:

q:
If the system of equations

x+y+az=b

2x+5y+2z=6

x+2y+3z=3

has infinitely many solutions, then 2a+3b is equal to
question:
If the system of equations

x+y+az=b

2x+5y+2z=6

x+2y+3z=3

has infinitely many solutions, then 2a+3b is equal to
solution:
Given system of equations are

x+y+az=b.....i

2x+5y+2z=6.....ii

x+2y+3z=3.....iii

It is given that the system of equations have infinite solutions.

⇒∆=0

⇒11a252123=0

⇒115-4-16-2+a4-5=0

⇒a=7 

Also, we have ⇒∆1=0

⇒b1a652323=0

⇒b15-4-118-6+712-15=0

⇒b=3

∴a=7, b=3 

∴2a+3b=14+9=23

Hence, this is the correct option.

q:
If the system of linear equations $2 x+2 y+3 z=a$

$3 x-y+5 z=b$

$x-3 y+2 z=c$

where, $a, b,$ care non-zero real numbers, has more than onc solution, then
question:
If the system of linear equations $2 x+2 y+3 z=a$

$3 x-y+5 z=b$

$x-3 y+2 z=c$

where, $a, b,$ care non-zero real numbers, has more than onc solution, then
solution:
$\because$ System of equations has more than one solution

$\therefore \Delta=\Delta_{1}=\Delta_{2}=\Delta_{3}=0$ for infinite solution

$\begin{aligned}

\Delta_{1} &=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}

a & 2 & 3 \\

b & -1 & 5 \\

c & -3 & 2

\end{array}\right|=a(13)+2(5 c-2 b)+3(-3 b+c) \\

&=13 a-13 b+13 c=0

\end{aligned}$

i.c, $a-b+c=0$

or $\quad b-c-a=0$

q:
Let the system of linear equations

–x+2y-9z=7

-x+3y+7z=9

-2x+y+5z=8

-3x+y+13z=λ

has a unique solution x=α, y=β, z=γ. Then the distance of the point α, β, γ from the plane 2x-2y+z=λ is
question:
Let the system of linear equations

–x+2y-9z=7

-x+3y+7z=9

-2x+y+5z=8

-3x+y+13z=λ

has a unique solution x=α, y=β, z=γ. Then the distance of the point α, β, γ from the plane 2x-2y+z=λ is
solution:
Given,

System of linear equation,

–x+2y-9z=7 .......i

–x+3y+7z=9 .......ii

–2x+y+5z=8 ......iii

–3x+y+13z=λ ......iv

Now solving, equations i, ii & iii we get,

x=–3, y=2, z=0

Now substituting in equation iv we get,

3×3+2=λ

⇒λ=11

Now finding the distance of Point –3, 2, 0 from the plane 2x-2y+z=11 we get,

d=-6-4-1122+22+1=213=7

q: If $D=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1+x & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 1+y\end{array}\right|$ for $x \neq 0, y \neq 0$ then $D$ is
question: If $D=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1+x & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 1+y\end{array}\right|$ for $x \neq 0, y \neq 0$ then $D$ is
solution:
$D=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 1+x & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 1+y\end{array}\right|$
$\mathrm{C}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_2-\mathrm{C}_1$ \& $\mathrm{C}_3 \rightarrow \mathrm{C}_3-\mathrm{C}_1$
$\left|\begin{array}{lll}
1 & 0 & 0 \\
1 & x & 0 \\
1 & 0 & y
\end{array}\right|=x y$.
Hence $D$ is divisible by both $x$ and $y$.

subject: Mathematics
q:
If the system of equations
$x + 5y + 6z = 4$,
$2x + 3y + 4z = 7$,
$x + 6y + az = b$
has infinitely many solutions, then the point $(a, b)$ lies on the line
question:
If the system of equations
$x + 5y + 6z = 4$,
$2x + 3y + 4z = 7$,
$x + 6y + az = b$
has infinitely many solutions, then the point $(a, b)$ lies on the line
solution:
For the given system of equations to have infinitely many solutions, the determinant of the coefficient matrix must be zero, $\Delta = 0$.

$\Delta = \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 5 & 6 \\ 2 & 3 & 4 \\ 1 & 6 & a \end{vmatrix} = 0$

Expanding along the first row:

$1(3a - 24) - 5(2a - 4) + 6(12 - 3) = 0$

$3a - 24 - 10a + 20 + 54 = 0$

$-7a + 50 = 0 \Rightarrow a = \dfrac{50}{7}$

For infinitely many solutions, we must also have $\Delta_z = 0$.

$\Delta_z = \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 5 & 4 \\ 2 & 3 & 7 \\ 1 & 6 & b \end{vmatrix} = 0$

Expanding along the first row:

$1(3b - 42) - 5(2b - 7) + 4(12 - 3) = 0$

$3b - 42 - 10b + 35 + 36 = 0$

$-7b + 29 = 0 \Rightarrow b = \dfrac{29}{7}$

The point $(a, b)$ is $\left(\dfrac{50}{7}, \dfrac{29}{7}\right)$.

Checking the given options, we evaluate $a - b$:

$a - b = \dfrac{50}{7} - \dfrac{29}{7} = \dfrac{21}{7} = 3$

Therefore, the point $(a, b)$ lies on the line $x - y = 3$.

Answer: $x - y = 3$

subject: Mathematics
q:
If the system of equations
$$
\begin{aligned}
& x+y+z=6 \\
& x+2 y+3 z=10 \\
& x+2 y+\lambda z=0
\end{aligned}
$$
has a unique solution, then $\lambda$ is not equal to
question:
If the system of equations
$$
\begin{aligned}
& x+y+z=6 \\
& x+2 y+3 z=10 \\
& x+2 y+\lambda z=0
\end{aligned}
$$
has a unique solution, then $\lambda$ is not equal to
solution:
Given system of equations is
$$
\begin{aligned}
& x+y+z=6 \\
& x+2 y+3 z=10 \\
& x+2 y+\lambda z=0
\end{aligned}
$$
It has unique solution.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore\left|\begin{array}{lll}
1 & 1 & 1 \\
1 & 2 & 3 \\
1 & 2 & \lambda
\end{array}\right| \neq 0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 1(2 \lambda-6)-1(\lambda-3)+1(2-2) \neq 0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda-6-\lambda+3 \neq 0 \Rightarrow \lambda-3 \neq 0 \Rightarrow \lambda \neq 3
\end{aligned}
$$

q:
The system of linear equations

3x-2y-kz=10

2x-4y-2z=6

x+2y-z=5 m
is inconsistent if :
question:
The system of linear equations

3x-2y-kz=10

2x-4y-2z=6

x+2y-z=5 m
is inconsistent if :
solution:
For inconsistent system of equation the conditions should be ∆=0 and at least one of the ∆x or ∆y or ∆z should not be equal to zero.

Now,

Δ=3-2-k2-4-212-1=0

⇒24+20-k8=0⇒k=3

And,

Δx=10-2-36-4-25 m2-1

⇒∆x=108-2-10 m+6-312+20 m

⇒∆x=84-5m

∆x≠0⇒m≠45

Δy=310-326-215 m-1

=3-6+10 m+100-310 m-6

=0

Δz=3-2102-46125m

⇒Δz=3-20m-12+210m-6+104+4

⇒Δz=-60m-36+20m-12+80

⇒Δz=-40m+32

⇒∆z=84-5m

∆z≠0⇒m≠45

q:
If the system of equations

kx+y+2z=1

3x-y-2z=2

-2x-2y-4z=3

has infinitely many solutions, then k is equal to ______ .
question:
If the system of equations

kx+y+2z=1

3x-y-2z=2

-2x-2y-4z=3

has infinitely many solutions, then k is equal to ______ .
solution:
If the system of equations

P1: kx+y+2z=1

P2: 3x-y-2z=2

P3: -2x-2y-4z=3

Which are equivalent to equations of planes.

We observe 5P2-P1=3P3

So, 15-K=-6

⇒ K=21

q: The number of values of $\mathrm{k}$ for which the linear equations $4 x+k y+2 z=0 ; k x+4 y+z=0 ; 2 x+2 y+z=0$ possess a non-zero solution is
question: The number of values of $\mathrm{k}$ for which the linear equations $4 x+k y+2 z=0 ; k x+4 y+z=0 ; 2 x+2 y+z=0$ possess a non-zero solution is
solution: $\left|\begin{array}{lll}4 & k & 2 \\ k & 4 & 1 \\ 2 & 2 & 1\end{array}\right|=0 \Rightarrow k^2-6 k+8=0 \Rightarrow k=4,2$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $\alpha \beta \gamma=45 ; \alpha, \beta, \gamma \in \mathbb{R}$. If $x(\alpha, 1,2)+y(1, \beta, 2)+z(2,3, \gamma)=(0,0,0)$ for some $x, y, z \in \mathbb{R}, x y z \neq 0$, then $6 \alpha+4 \beta+\gamma$ is equal to _______
question: Let $\alpha \beta \gamma=45 ; \alpha, \beta, \gamma \in \mathbb{R}$. If $x(\alpha, 1,2)+y(1, \beta, 2)+z(2,3, \gamma)=(0,0,0)$ for some $x, y, z \in \mathbb{R}, x y z \neq 0$, then $6 \alpha+4 \beta+\gamma$ is equal to _______
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \alpha \beta \gamma=45, \alpha \beta \gamma \in \mathrm{R} \\ & \mathrm{x}(\alpha, 1,2)+\mathrm{y}(1, \beta, 2)+\mathrm{z}(2,3, \gamma)=(0,0,0) \\ & \mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}, \mathrm{z} \in \mathrm{R}, \mathrm{xyz} \neq 0 \\ & \alpha \mathrm{x}+\mathrm{y}+2 \mathrm{z}=0 \\ & \mathrm{x}+\beta \mathrm{y}+3 \mathrm{z}=0 \\ & 2 \mathrm{x}+2 \mathrm{y}+\gamma \mathrm{z}=0 \\ & \mathrm{xyz} \neq 0 \Rightarrow \text { non-trivial } \\ & \left|\begin{array}{lll}\alpha & 1 & 2 \\ 1 & \beta & 3 \\ 2 & 2 & \gamma\end{array}\right|=0\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \alpha(\beta \gamma-6)-1(\gamma-6)+2(2-2 \beta)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \alpha \beta \gamma-6 \alpha-\gamma+6+4-4 \beta=0 \\ & \Rightarrow 6 \alpha+4 \beta+\gamma=55\end{aligned}$

question: The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mtable columnalign="left"><mtr><mtd><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></mtd><mtd><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></mtd><mtd><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></mtd><mtd><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn></mtd><mtd><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math> is
solution:
R2→R2-R1 and R3→R3-R1

Δ=a+1a+2a+21a+2a+3-a-110a2+7a+12-a2-3a-220

=a2+3a+2a+212a+2104a+1020

=4a+2-4 a-10

subject: Mathematics
q:
If the system of equations
$\begin{aligned}
& 2 x+7 y+\lambda z=3 \\
& 3 x+2 y+5 z=4 \\
& x+\mu y+32 z=-1
\end{aligned}$
has infinitely many solutions, then $(\lambda-\mu)$ is equal to________
question:
If the system of equations
$\begin{aligned}
& 2 x+7 y+\lambda z=3 \\
& 3 x+2 y+5 z=4 \\
& x+\mu y+32 z=-1
\end{aligned}$
has infinitely many solutions, then $(\lambda-\mu)$ is equal to________
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{D}=\mathrm{D}_1=\mathrm{D}_2=\mathrm{D}_3=0 \\ & \mathrm{D}_3=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}2 & 7 & 3 \\ 3 & 2 & 4 \\ 1 & \mu & -1\end{array}\right|=0 \Rightarrow \mu=-39 \\ & \mathrm{D}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}2 & 7 & \lambda \\ 3 & 2 & 5 \\ 1 & -39 & 32\end{array}\right|=0 \Rightarrow \lambda=-1 \\ & \lambda-\mu=38\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let M and m respectively be the maximum and the minimum values of
\(f(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1+\sin ^2 x & \cos ^2 x & 4 \sin 4 x \\
\sin ^2 x & 1+\cos ^2 x & 4 \sin 4 x \\
\sin ^2 x & \cos ^2 x & 1+4 \sin 4 x
\end{array}\right|, x \in \mathrm{R}\)
Then \(M^4-m^4\) is equal to :
question:
Let M and m respectively be the maximum and the minimum values of
\(f(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1+\sin ^2 x & \cos ^2 x & 4 \sin 4 x \\
\sin ^2 x & 1+\cos ^2 x & 4 \sin 4 x \\
\sin ^2 x & \cos ^2 x & 1+4 \sin 4 x
\end{array}\right|, x \in \mathrm{R}\)
Then \(M^4-m^4\) is equal to :
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1+\sin ^2 x & \cos ^2 x & 4 \sin 4 x \\
\sin ^2 x & 1+\cos ^2 x & 4 \sin 4 x \\
\sin ^2 x & \cos ^2 x & 1+4 \sin 4 x
\end{array}\right|, x \in R \\
& R_2 \rightarrow R_2-R_1 \& R_3 \rightarrow R_3 \rightarrow R_1 \\
& f(x)\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1+\sin ^2 x & \cos ^2 x & 4 \sin 4 x \\
-1 & 1 & 0 \\
-1 & 0 & 1
\end{array}\right|
\end{aligned}$

Expand about $\mathrm{R}_1$, use get
$f(x)=2+4 \sin 4 x$
$\therefore \mathrm{M}=$ max value of $\mathrm{f}(\mathrm{x})=6$
$m=\min$ value of $f(x)=-2$
$\therefore \mathrm{m}^4-\mathrm{M}^4=1280$

subject: Mathematics
q:
The sum of the real roots of the equation
x-6-12-3xx-3-32xx+2=0, is equal to:
question:
The sum of the real roots of the equation
x-6-12-3xx-3-32xx+2=0, is equal to:
solution:
Given x-6-12-3xx-3-32xx+2=0

On expansion, we get x-3xx+2-2xx-3+62x+2+3x-3-14x-9x=0

⇒x-3x2-6x-2x2+6x+62x+4+3x-9-1-5x=0

⇒-5x3+30x-30+5x=0

⇒5x3-35x+30=0

⇒x3-7x+6=0

⇒x-1x-2x+3

⇒x=1 or x=2 or x=-3.

Here all roots 1, 2, -3 are real, hence the sum of the real roots is 1+2-3=0.

subject: Mathematics
q:
If $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a-b-c & 2 a & 2 a \\ 2 b & b-c-a & 2 b \\ 2 c & 2 c & c-a-b\end{array}\right|$

$=(a+b+c)(x+a+b+c)^{2}, x \neq 0$ and $a+b+c \neq 0,$ then $x$

is equal to
question:
If $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a-b-c & 2 a & 2 a \\ 2 b & b-c-a & 2 b \\ 2 c & 2 c & c-a-b\end{array}\right|$

$=(a+b+c)(x+a+b+c)^{2}, x \neq 0$ and $a+b+c \neq 0,$ then $x$

is equal to
solution:
$\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a-b-c & 2 a & 2 a \\ 2 b & b-c-a & 2 b \\ 2 c & 2 c & c-a-b\end{array}\right|$

$R_{1} \rightarrow R_{1}+R_{2}+R$

$\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a+b+c & a+b+c & a+b+c \\ 2 b & b-c-a & 2 b \\ 2 c & 2 c & c-a-b\end{array}\right|$

$=(a+b+c)\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 1 & 1 \\ 2 b & b-c-a & 2 b \\ 2 c & 2 c & c-a-b\end{array}\right|$

$\begin{array}{l}

C_{1} \rightarrow C_{1}-C_{y}, C_{2} \rightarrow C_{2}-C_{3} \\

\Delta=(a+b+c)\left|\begin{array}{ccc}

0 & 0 & 1 \\

0 & -b-c-a & 2 b \\

c+a+b & c+a+b & c-a-b

\end{array}\right|

\end{array}$

$=(a+b+c)(a+b+c)^{2}$

Hence, $x=-2(a+b+c)$
